topics.title: A Framework Is Not a Values Poster: What Teams Should Document
topics.dek: If it does not change a ticket, a test, or a sign-off, it is wallpaper. Here is the minimum structure that counts as a framework for shipping AI.

sections.heading: Three artifacts that make it real
paragraphs: A useful ethical AI framework ties abstract principles to decisions someone actually makes: who approves a new model or feature, what evidence they need, and what happens when a check fails. Without those hooks, "be fair" and "respect privacy" are slogans, not engineering constraints.

Start with a one-page decision map: intake (idea or model change), owners (named roles, not "the org"), required reviews (bias, privacy, safety, human oversight), and exit criteria for production. That map is your framework in practice; the glossy diagram is optional.

sections.heading: Why posters still appear
paragraphs: Leadership often asks for visible ethics commitments before process exists. That is fine as long as you file the poster next to a living checklist in Jira or your release tool. The LinkedIn audience for ethical AI frameworks usually wants both: language they can rally around and mechanics they can audit.

topics.title: From NIST AI RMF to a Two-Pizza Team Backlog
topics.dek: Map Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage to concrete stories so a small team does not drown in abstraction.

sections.heading: Govern without a bureaucracy
paragraphs: Pick one accountable owner for AI risk in the product line, one deputy, and a single escalation path. Document that in your wiki and link it from your repo README. That satisfies the spirit of Govern faster than a twelve-person council that never meets.

sections.heading: Map and Measure as release gates
paragraphs: Turn Map into an inventory: data sources, model versions, downstream users, and known failure modes. Turn Measure into a short list of metrics or qualitative checks that run before major releases—aligned with whatever you already track for quality and safety.

Manage is your backlog: mitigations, accepted risks with expiry dates, and incidents that feed the next Map update. Revisit the map when the model, data, or regulation changes—not only at annual compliance time.
